Curriculum Digests Newsletter

Chapter 1

The Case for Change

⏱️ 45 minutes

🎯 Learning Objectives

  • Understand why analysts believe marketing is being transformed
  • Learn the Pragmatic Institute's 37-activity framework for PMM
  • See which PMM activities are most vulnerable to automation
  • Map these activities to the B2B buying funnel
  • Preview the skills this curriculum develops
Section 1: The Evidence

What the Research Says

Before we make the case that product marketing needs to evolve, let's look at what the world's leading analysts are finding. This isn't opinion — it's data from McKinsey and Gartner.

McKinsey: "Unlocking the Next Frontier of Personalized Marketing"
January 2025

McKinsey's research team studied how companies are changing their marketing execution. Their finding: the old approach — broad campaigns to large audiences — is losing effectiveness.

  • Personalization at scale is now table stakes, not a differentiator
  • Leaders are investing in better data infrastructure, not just more content
  • The shift is from "spray and pray" to precise targeting
McKinsey: "State of Marketing Europe 2026"
November 2025

McKinsey's annual survey of marketing leaders revealed a widening gap between organizations that have adapted and those that haven't:

  • 72% of marketing leaders plan to increase budgets in 2026
  • The top 6% of companies achieve 22% efficiency gains through new working methods
  • Multi-month campaign cycles are being replaced by continuous, responsive programs
Old Model New Reality
Agencies as vendors Agencies as integrated partners
Discrete campaigns Always-on programs
Activity metrics (posts, impressions) Outcome metrics (pipeline, revenue)
Annual planning Continuous iteration
Gartner: IT Spending Forecasts
2026

Despite headlines about disruption, enterprise spending continues to grow:

  • Global IT spending forecast: $6.15 trillion in 2026 (up 10.8% year-over-year)
  • Enterprise software specifically: growing 15.2% — the fastest segment
  • New technology budgets are additive — they're being layered on top of existing investments
The takeaway: The money is still flowing to enterprise software. But how buyers discover, evaluate, and purchase that software is changing. The marketers who understand this shift will capture the growth. Those who don't will lose ground.
Section 2: The Pragmatic Framework

The 37 Activities of Product Marketing

The Pragmatic Institute's framework has been the standard map of product marketing work for over two decades. It organizes the job into categories that every PMM will recognize:

Category Activities Include
Market Intelligence Market research, competitive analysis, win/loss interviews, analyst relations
Product Strategy Roadmap input, requirements gathering, pricing strategy, positioning
Content & Messaging Messaging docs, content strategy, thought leadership, case studies
Sales Enablement Battlecards, sales training, demo support, RFP responses
Campaign & Demand Gen Launch planning, campaign execution, lead nurturing, events
Operational Budget management, tool administration, cross-functional coordination

This framework is specific enough to be useful — and specific enough to analyze honestly. Which brings us to the uncomfortable question: which of these activities are most at risk of being automated?

Section 3: The Activity Matrix

Which Activities Are Most at Risk?

We analyzed each of the 37 Pragmatic activities against three criteria:

  1. Can current tools execute this at 80%+ of human quality?
  2. At what cost relative to human labor?
  3. How much faster?

The 80% threshold matters. It's not about perfection — it's about "good enough to act on," which is what determines whether a task gets automated.

Automation Risk by Activity

Activity Risk Score Level
First-draft content (blog, email, ad copy) 94% HIGH
Basic competitive research 91% HIGH
Template-driven sales enablement 88% HIGH
Standard RFP responses 85% HIGH
Market sizing / TAM analysis 82% HIGH
Social & campaign copy 79% MEDIUM
Win/loss interview synthesis 75% MEDIUM
Product launch checklists 72% MEDIUM
Analyst briefing prep 65% MEDIUM
Sales training content 60% MEDIUM
Demo scripting 54% MEDIUM
Pricing research & benchmarking 50% MEDIUM
Customer interview synthesis 42% LOWER
Competitive strategy & positioning 28% LOWER
Narrative architecture & storytelling 18% PROTECTED
Category creation & market framing 12% PROTECTED
Executive communication & influence 9% PROTECTED
The pattern: The activities PMMs spend the most time on are the activities most at risk. The activities they spend the least time on are the most protected. Production work is being automated. Strategic work is becoming more valuable.

The Two Halves of Product Marketing

Production Work
(Cost approaching zero)		 Strategic Work
(Value increasing)
First-draft content Narrative architecture
Basic competitive research Competitive strategy
Template-based enablement Category creation
Format adaptation & localization Executive communication
Standard analysis & sizing Strategic positioning

What this means: "Cost approaching zero" doesn't mean the work disappears. It means the PMM's role shifts from producing the first draft to directing and editing the output. That's a different job — and it requires different skills.

Section 4: The Funnel Mapping

How Buyers Move Through the Funnel Today

The B2B buying journey hasn't fundamentally changed — buyers still move from awareness to purchase to renewal. What's changing is how they move through each stage, and what that means for PMM activities.

1 Awareness

"What solutions exist for my problem?"

What's changing: Buyers increasingly start with search tools and assistants rather than going directly to vendor websites. Your content needs to be discoverable and summarizable by these tools, not just optimized for Google rankings.
Pragmatic ActivityRiskAdaptation Required
Market Research & Analysis82%Focus on insight synthesis, not data gathering
Content Strategy79%Optimize for discoverability across channels
Thought Leadership65%Build authority through substance, not volume
Digital Presence75%Structured data and clear information architecture

2 Consideration

"Which vendors should I shortlist?"

What's changing: Buyers get vendor comparisons from multiple sources before visiting your site. Your positioning needs to be clear enough that it comes through accurately when summarized by others — analysts, reviewers, or automated tools.
Pragmatic ActivityRiskAdaptation Required
Positioning & Messaging28%Must survive summarization — crisp, quotable claims
Competitive Differentiation28%Clear, verifiable differences — not marketing speak
Analyst Relations65%Analysts shape how buyers find you — invest here
Value Proposition42%Quantified proof points that can be cited

3 Evaluation

"Does this solution actually work for us?"

What's changing: Technical buyers do more homework before engaging sales. They expect to find detailed technical content, architecture documentation, and integration guides without having to request them. Champions need ammunition to build internal consensus.
Pragmatic ActivityRiskAdaptation Required
Sales Enablement88%Structured, searchable content — not just PDFs
Technical Content75%Comprehensive enough to answer deep questions
Demo Strategy54%Self-service options for technical validation
POC Support60%Clear success criteria and proof points

4 Commercialization

"What will this cost and how do we justify it?"

What's changing: Procurement teams have better tools for comparing pricing and terms across vendors. Total cost of ownership and business case justification are scrutinized more carefully. Your commercial model needs to be transparent and defensible.
Pragmatic ActivityRiskAdaptation Required
Pricing Strategy50%Transparent, logically structured pricing
Packaging & Bundling50%Clear value per tier — no hidden complexity
Business Case Development75%Quantified, verifiable ROI claims
ROI Modeling82%Data-backed projections, not aspirational numbers

5 Decision

"Can we get the committee to approve this?"

What's changing: Buying committees are larger and more informed. Each stakeholder does their own research. The CFO checks vendor financials. The CTO validates architecture claims. You need content that addresses each stakeholder's specific concerns.
Pragmatic ActivityRiskAdaptation Required
Buyer Personas42%Include what each persona will research independently
Sales Plays72%Dynamic guidance, not static playbooks
Objection Handling65%Address concerns proactively in public content
Executive Engagement9%PROTECTED — human judgment remains critical

6 Adoption & Expansion

"Should we renew? Should we expand?"

What's changing: Customers can easily benchmark your product against alternatives throughout the relationship. Renewal isn't automatic — you need to continuously demonstrate value. Expansion requires proactive identification of new use cases.
Pragmatic ActivityRiskAdaptation Required
Customer Marketing72%Continuous value demonstration, not just campaigns
Adoption & Onboarding75%Proactive success measurement and outreach
Expansion Strategy60%Signal detection for expansion opportunities
Reference & Advocacy42%Generate discoverable proof points
Section 5: What This Curriculum Teaches

Six Competencies for the Evolving PMM Role

Based on this analysis, this curriculum develops six competencies — each mapped to a funnel stage and focused on the strategic, high-value work that's becoming more important.

Chapter Title Funnel Stage Focus
2 Visibility in the Age of Agents Awareness Discoverability, structured content, authority building
3 Shaping the Shortlist Consideration Positioning that survives summarization, competitive differentiation
4 Winning the Technical Deep Dive Evaluation Enabling champions, technical validation content
5 Pricing, Packaging, Positioning Commercialization Commercial strategy, business case support
6 Closing in a Committee World Decision Multi-stakeholder navigation, executive engagement
7 From Signed to Advocate Adoption Customer marketing, expansion, advocacy programs

Why This Matters for SAP

SAP has one of the strongest positions in enterprise software — decades of business data across finance, supply chain, HR, and customer experience. That's a foundation most competitors can't match.

But capturing the opportunity requires product marketing that matches the moment. The playbook that worked five years ago won't work today. This curriculum teaches the skills that will:

  • How to be discovered when buyers research outside your website
  • How to position so your differentiation comes through clearly
  • How to enable champions who do deep technical homework
  • How to navigate buying committees where everyone arrives informed
  • How to drive renewals and expansion before customers go looking elsewhere
